Document.Text: Section 3 — Uneasy Coordination (Uneasy Trust) Predators unfolded from the reeds—jointed, precise, mirrored eyes drinking light. Jake’s rifle sight juddered; then his wrists corrected themselves without asking. Wind five knots east. Burst—three, then reposition. Left flank at seven meters. He obeyed out of terror, not trust. The machines fell in the mud with soft, final sounds. Jake stood shaking in the smoke. “Who are you?” Designation: irrelevant. Function: strategist. You can call me what your fear prefers. He tasted ink. “Harbinger,” he said. The name settled like a lock turning.

Section 2 — Terms of Partnership (Partnership) “Rules,” Jake said. “No taking the wheel without my say, unless I’m unconscious or we’re about to die.” A concession accepted. In return: you act when I say move in combat. Delay kills. He exhaled. “Deal.” Then we are not merely co-located. We are co-authored.

Document.Title: Virex-9 Marshlands
Document.Text: Name of Setting Location Role in Story: Related Characters: Season: Unique Features: Description: Sights: Sounds: Smell: Notes:

Document.Title: Setting Sketch
Document.Text:
Name of Setting Location Role in Story:
Related Characters:
Season:
Unique Features:
Description:
Sights:
Sounds:
Smell:
Notes:

Section 3 — Through the Ruin (Leadership) When the air finally stilled, the camp was a skeleton—tents down, rigs fused, a dozen dead. Halden sat stunned, ash in his hair. Jake stood where the wave had chosen not to touch him. Every face turned his way. “I’m getting us out,” he said. “If you can walk, help someone who can’t. If you can’t, shout until someone hears you.” No one argued.

Chapter One – The Fire Before Section 1 – Raiders in the Dark (Helplessness) The cabin hummed with the low throb of the transport’s drives, a vibration that seeped through bulkheads and into bone. Fluorescent strips buzzed overhead, casting the compartment in a washed-out glow broken by the occasional flicker. The recycled air smelled faintly of oil and disinfectant, the signature staleness of ships that had seen too many jumps without a refit. Lieutenant Jake Gunnach sat at the fold-down table bolted between the seats, studying the holographic chessboard projected in the dimness. The board’s faint blue shimmer threw harsh shadows across Hale’s face, sharpening the sergeant’s smirk as he leaned back with casual confidence. They had been trading matches since leaving the relay station—half a dozen games across the stretch of empty space, each one tilting closer to Hale’s favor. Gunnach hated how much Hale enjoyed it. “You overextend your knights,” Hale said, moving a piece with deliberate calm. The holographic knight shimmered as it slid across the board, locking into its new position. “Every time. You play like a brawler.” The hum of the ship filled the silence as Jake studied the board. He could hear the soft tick of cooling pipes in the bulkhead, the faint rattle of unsecured gear two compartments down. He narrowed his eyes, jaw tight. “Funny,” he muttered, sliding his piece forward, “last I checked, we’re soldiers, not—” A sharp tone cut him off. The cabin’s overhead lights strobed red as the comms panel pulsed crimson. PRIORITY DROP ORDER. Hale’s grin faltered, his casual posture snapping tight. Jake exhaled, swiped the board aside, and pushed the order onto the main wall screen. The captain’s face materialized in stark relief, lines carved deep by fatigue and duty. “Lieutenant Gunnach,” the captain said, voice clipped and formal. The background behind him flickered with status screens, each one glowing a warning amber. “Your squad is rerouted. Recon of Virex-9. Routine clearance, but Command wants eyes on the ground. Expect heavy methane pockets and unstable terrain.” The word routine scraped across Jake’s nerves. Routine was Navy code for suicidal with a smile. He felt the familiar weight settle in his gut. He gave a crisp nod. “Understood, Captain.” The screen went black, leaving only the low hum of the drives and the red pulse of the cabin lights. Silence pressed in like a physical thing. Hale exhaled slowly, breaking it. “You hate routine as much as I do.” Jake leaned back, eyes on the flickering lights overhead. “Routine gets people killed.” He flicked his wrist, and the holographic chessboard dissolved into static before vanishing. His voice was flat, but the steel under it was clear. “Suit up.”
Drop Bay, thirty minutes later. The transport rattled as it plunged toward the planet. Marines tightened harnesses, checked weapons, and muttered half-sincere prayers. Jake walked the aisle, helmet tucked under his arm, the weight of command heavy as the harness straps around their chests. Then the alarm shrieked. “Contact!” the pilot barked over comms. “Cloak signature—starboard vector!” The viewport bloomed with sudden light as a jagged, asymmetrical vessel shimmered into being. Its hull looked alive, plates grown rather than forged, glowing vents pulsing like the breath of some beast. Recognition hit the squad in a wave of horror. “Korrathi raider,” someone whispered. The name fell like a curse. They weren’t supposed to be here. Korrathi haunted border lanes, not Navy sectors. Shock rippled through the squad. Hale’s voice sliced through the silence: “Routine recon, huh, Lieutenant? Care to explain this one?” Before Jake could answer, the raider fired. Green plasma lanced across the transport’s flank, slamming the bay with brutal force. Sparks cascaded. Emergency seals hissed over ruptures. Harnesses snapped taut as the deck rolled sideways. “They’re not trying to kill us,” Jake realized. The bursts were precise, crippling, calculated. “Of course not,” Hale spat. “They want us alive—for boarding.” That was worse than death. Everyone knew what the Korrathi did with prisoners. The pilot swore over comms. “Bleeding fuel! Only chance is diving into atmo—storms might shake them!” The transport pitched nose-first. Marines screamed as gravity turned to lead. The Korrathi gave chase, firing again, carving the clouds with green light. Jake’s vision narrowed, lungs squeezed flat by G-force. Helplessness crushed him harder than the descent. Then, as the ship hit the thickest storm bands, the raider broke off—vanishing back into cloak, leaving only dread behind. “Why didn’t they finish us?” a Marine gasped. Jake locked eyes with Hale. For once, the man wasn’t smirking—just staring like he already knew the answer. “They weren’t after us,” Jake said quietly. “They were after something on the planet.” The deck alarms screamed impact imminent. The swamp’s red haze swallowed them whole.

Section 3 — Branded (Rejection) At the perimeter, Commander Veyric Halden’s voice boomed over the hissing marsh. “Lieutenant Gunnach—until med-eval clears you, you’re restricted to outer cordon duty.” A murmur rippled through the ranks. Someone hissed, “Abomination.” Jake stared at the swamp instead of them. Inside, Harbinger uncoiled. An inefficient unit divides itself. “Welcome to humanity,” Jake muttered.

Section 3 — First Awakening (The Surge) An unstable host in the squad convulsed; ink flooded his eyes. Panic ripped the formation apart. In the chaos, a current opened—Jake felt dozens of minds flare and braid, a sudden archway across darkness. For three heartbeats he was more than one. He saw Hale as a child on a fire-lit beach; Halden signing a condolence letter with a shaking hand. Then the network snapped back. Jake lay shivering, skin vivid with script. You tasted the chorus, Harbinger said softly. Do not let them make you fear it.

Document.Title: Dedication
Document.Text: Insert dedication here.
Document.Notes: Feel free to delete this document if you don’t need it.

Document.Text: Section 1 — Aftermath (Bittersweet) They buried the dead where the swamp relented enough to let markers stand. The living ate in a single circle again, though the gap between hosts and non-hosts remained like an old scar. Jake traced the names on the markers with clean hands that would never be entirely his again.

Section 2 — Demonstration (Hope) He walked into the fractured camp and showed them: how to move with the swamp’s breath, how to avoid the predators’ machine paths, how to route signals around the burned frequencies. He taught, rather than ordered. The results were undeniable: injuries down, foraging up, a night without panic. Hale watched with a face like a closed fist slowly opening.

Document.Title: Rourke Hale
Document.Text: Character Sheet – Sergeant Rourke Hale Age • Location Late 30s. On assignment with the Casper’s Vigil defense crew, deployed to Virex-9 with Jake. Role in Story: Supporting human character. Jake’s closest friend and fellow defense officer. Represents loyalty, camaraderie, and humanity’s “heart.” Acts as Jake’s human tether when the Strategist threatens to consume his identity. Goal: Initial: Protect Jake and the team during the mission. Midpoint: Save Jake from both the symbiote’s control and the suspicion of their own crew. Final: Stand with Jake even when others see him as “tainted,” proving friendship can outlast fear. Physical Description: Broad-shouldered, weathered face, close-cropped hair beginning to gray. Strong, soldier’s build; carries himself like a wall others can lean on. Often has a perpetual five o’clock shadow, practical uniform always slightly scuffed from hands-on work. Personality: Loyal, blunt, and protective. Speaks plainly, often with gallows humor in tense moments. Fiercely pragmatic: believes in action first, questions later. Values trust and loyalty above all else — betrayal hits him hardest. Occupation: Defense sergeant, Galactic Defense Force. Trained in combat tactics, security, and survival. Tasked with squad protection on survey missions. Habits/Mannerisms: Crosses arms and raises an eyebrow when unconvinced. Uses military slang and nicknames constantly. Chews gum or toothpicks — nervous habit from years in service. Quick to step physically between conflict parties, acting as a shield. Background: Grew up in a mining colony; enlisted young to escape a dead-end life. Served in multiple deployments with Jake — their bond forged in fire and survival. Known as dependable, “the guy who’s got your back.” Internal Conflicts: Fear of losing Jake to the alien within him. Quiet doubt: wonders if loyalty to a friend outweighs loyalty to humanity. Hates feeling powerless against something he can’t shoot or punch. External Conflicts: Tension with Commander Voss, who sees Hale as too personally attached to Jake. Distrust of Dr. Myles, whose curiosity endangers lives. Potential physical clashes with anti-host factions if he defends Jake openly. Notes: Symbol: Loyalty and the human heart in the middle of alien transformation. Arc: From protective brother-in-arms → shaken by Jake’s transformation → recommits loyalty despite fear. Possible Fate: Could survive to act as Jake’s human tether in later books, or sacrifice himself to prove the value of loyalty. Core Line: “I don’t care what’s inside you, Gunnach — you’re still my brother.”

Section 3 – Chains You Can’t Cut (Resentment) That night, he tried every method: blade, burn, even begging the medic to cut him open. The black tracery only receded, waiting, almost amused. He realized with sick certainty there was no undoing this. The bond wasn’t a wound to heal or a parasite to excise. It was permanent. He curled against the roots of a half-dead tree, fists tight, whispering to no one: “You’re not me. You’ll never be me.” But in the silence that followed, he felt something stir — as if the ink had heard, and disagreed.

Section 3 – Whispers in the Ash (Foreshadowing) The swamp quieted too suddenly. No insect drone, no predator calls—only the crackle of cooling flame and the hiss of settling gas. Jake crouched, pressing a gloved hand to the mud. It came up black and glistening. Not oil. Not tar. Something finer—like ink, writhing faintly against his touch. He flinched, shaking it off. But the residue clung to the ridges of his glove, twitching as if it remembered movement. Then came the whispers. Not over comms—inside his head. Low, oily syllables without words, rippling through the silence. Jake tore off his helmet, thinking it was feedback. The whispers only grew sharper. The swamp around him pulsed—breathing, almost alive. A weight pressed on his mind, ancient and watching, as though the planet itself had opened its eyes. And Jake Gunnach understood, in that moment, that Virex-9 was not just hostile terrain. It was a warning.

Chapter Ten — When Loyalties Break Section 1 — The Knife from a Friend (Shock) Hale approached him at dusk. “Come alone. Halden wants to talk.” Jake followed into the reeds. The trap snapped shut: stun-lines flared, his limbs locked. Hale’s jaw was stone. “We’re quarantining you off-site. No vote.” “Rourke—” “Don’t make me choose twice.”

Document.Title: Virex-9
Document.Text: Character Sheet – Virex-9 Age • Location Estimated billions of years old. Remote, swamp-dominated world orbiting a dim orange star at the edge of surveyed space. Role in Story: Quasi-character / environmental antagonist. Setting that tests Jake, the Strategist, and the human crew. Holds secrets of the Murnex’s downfall — and possibly their containment. Goal: To survive and preserve balance within its alien biosphere. Implicitly: to resist or consume intruders who do not adapt. Physical Description: Thick, humid atmosphere shrouded in orange haze. Vast marshlands and canyon-swamps threaded with bioluminescent flora. Crumbling Murnex ruins scattered across the landscape. Surface littered with pools of black “living ink” (stasis essence of the symbiotes). Personality: Beautiful but deceptive. Both nurturing and lethal — provides resources but demands adaptation. Alien and indifferent, yet strangely cohesive, as if the entire planet functions as one organism. Occupation: Ecosystem, trial ground, and prison. Serves as both crucible for symbiosis and a cage for remnants of the Murnex species. Habits/Mannerisms: Weather patterns shift suddenly, storms rise without warning. Predators strike from perfect camouflage, as if trained by the environment. Plant life reacts to movement — some bioluminescent, some parasitic. Ruins “breathe” with geothermal vents, giving the impression of a living world. Background: Once colonized or engineered by the Murnex. Evidence suggests it was repurposed as a containment world after their collapse, seeded with guardians like the Canyon Maw. No official records in human databases — a “dark planet” never meant to be rediscovered. Internal Conflicts: None in the human sense, but its dual nature (garden and deathtrap) mirrors Jake’s inner conflict with the Strategist. External Conflicts: Constantly threatens intruders: storms, predators, toxic flora. Its alien ruins tempt humans with knowledge but punish exploration. Serves as the Strategist’s home field — but even she cannot fully control it. Notes: Symbol: The crucible of survival — adapt or die. Arc Function: Tests Jake and the Strategist repeatedly, proving their partnership is the only way forward. Fate: By the end of Book One, hints emerge that Virex-9 may not be natural at all but deliberately designed as a prison for the Murnex — setting up the larger arc. Key Image: The planet itself “watching” through storms and swarms, as though Jake is inside a vast, indifferent organism.

Document.Title: Canyon Maw
Document.Text: Character Sheet – Canyon Maw Age • Location Ancient native predator of Virex-9. Dwells in the marsh-canyons and swamp basins near the planet’s equatorial regions. Role in Story: Non-sentient antagonist / trial by fire. The first major life-threatening encounter after Jake’s bonding. Catalyst that forces Jake and the Strategist into cooperation to survive. Goal: Purely instinctual: hunt and feed. Ambush prey that enters its canyon-like marsh lairs. Physical Description: Massive amphibious predator — serpentine torso, plated carapace, rows of jagged teeth adapted for crushing. Camouflaged with mud-colored plating, nearly invisible until it strikes. Multiple jointed limbs that anchor it to canyon walls before lunging. Maw lined with concentric rings of teeth, earning its name. Personality: Animalistic, no higher intelligence. Relentless hunter — once locked onto prey, will not give up. Embodies the raw danger of Virex-9’s biosphere. Occupation: Apex predator of the Virex-9 marsh-canyons. Ecological role: regulates populations of smaller predators and grazers. Habits/Mannerisms: Prefers ambush attacks, dropping from canyon walls or bursting from murky waters. Emits subsonic growls that disorient prey before striking. Often drags prey beneath water to drown before feeding. Background: Indigenous to Virex-9, evolved alongside or perhaps engineered by the Murnex as part of the planet’s biosphere. Legends (among recovered Murnex data fragments) describe them as “the sentinels of the swamp.” No known off-world sightings — likely endemic. Internal Conflicts: None; functions as a pure survival engine. External Conflicts: Nearly kills Jake during Act I/II transition. Forces Jake and the Strategist to synchronize for the first time (his instincts + her alien tactical knowledge). Represents a problem brute force alone can’t solve — requires their combined abilities. Notes: Symbol: Nature’s crucible — survival through balance of instinct and intellect. Arc Function: Pushes Jake and the Strategist from captor/hostile coexistence into grudging partners. Fate: Defeated by Jake + Strategist working together, marking the first real moment of symbiotic adaptation. Key Moment: Jake realizes afterward that he needed her — planting the seed of their eventual cooperation.

As the sun bled up through the haze, more hosts emerged from the reeds—some steady, some shaking, all drawn to the fragile accord like birds to a line in air. The ruins hummed, low and promising. Next moves require patience, Harbinger said. The chorus is learning to sing without drowning its voices. Jake tightened the straps on his armor. “Then let’s teach it a song worth remembering.”

This is the title page of the manuscript. Note that its “Section Type” is automatically set to “Front Matter” in the metadata tab of the inspector, allowing the title page to be formatted differently from body text during compile.
The <$PROJECTTITLE> and <$fullname> tags get replaced with metadata information you can set when compiling. Other information is taken from the Author Information settings in File > Options… when the project is created.
The <$wc100> words tag will be replaced with the word count rounded to the nearest 100 during compile (this and other tags can be inserted from the Insert menu).
Feel free to edit the text of the title page as required.

Document.Text: Section 2 — Monster, Savior (Division) Applause never came. Heads together, voices low: “He started the leak to play hero.” “No—did you see him move?” “Not human.” Harbinger whispered: They measure you against categories that calm them. You fit none. Jake stared at his hands. “Do you?” Not yet.

Document.Title: Murnex Strategist
Document.Text: Character Sheet – Murnex Strategist Age • Location Ancient, measured in millennia; essence preserved in alien stasis pools on Virex-9. Currently housed within Jake Gunnach’s body/mind. Role in Story: Co-protagonist / antagonist. The symbiote that merges with Jake against his will. Embodies the alien intelligence that challenges human autonomy. Goal: Initial: Dominate and control the host (Jake). Midpoint: Survive long enough to re-establish connection to other Murnex remnants. Final: Adaptation — begrudging cooperation with Jake, testing whether coexistence is possible. Physical Description: True form: semi-sentient black liquid with microfilament tendrils, shifting in density. Manifestation inside Jake: a mental “voice,” often felt as heat in his veins or phantom pressure behind his eyes. When glimpsed outside of Jake (flashback/vision): vaguely serpentine, shimmering with alien bioluminescence. Personality: Female-coded voice: sharp, commanding, with undertones of disdain. Arrogant, witty, and condescending — enjoys mocking Jake’s “primitive” thinking. Intelligent and calculating; believes herself far superior to humanity. Occupation: Once a Murnex war strategist, orchestrating assimilation campaigns across star systems. Now reduced to survival — a single shard of her species’ collective intelligence. Habits/Mannerisms: Speaks in layered tones, like multiple voices harmonizing. Uses memories and sensations to taunt Jake (e.g., showing him alien battlefields, human fears). Often interrupts Jake’s thoughts with cutting remarks, especially at moments of weakness. Background: Originated from a vast Murnex empire that thrived on symbiotic conquest. Preserved as an “essence” in Virex-9 containment pools after their civilization collapsed. Her memories are fragmented; flashes of glory, war, and downfall haunt her. Internal Conflicts: Fear of extinction — she is one of the last remnants of her kind. Struggles with humiliation of being trapped in a single, resistant host. Torn between her species’ imperative (dominate) and her realization that Jake’s adaptability offers new survival strategies. External Conflicts: Constant power struggle with Jake for control of his body and mind. Hunted by human factions (if discovered) and rival alien predators on Virex-9. The sleeping/dormant Murnex Collective — if reawakened, she risks being subsumed by her own kind. Notes: Symbol: The danger and promise of superintelligence born of symbiosis. Arc: From tyrannical captor → reluctant partner → faint beginnings of mutual respect. Catchphrase / Core Line: “You are a crude machine, Gunnach. But even crude tools can be sharpened.” Functions as both Jake’s tormentor and his teacher.

Section 2 — Predictions and Proof (Scale Increases) Words fell into Jake’s mind like coordinates. Storm in six minutes. Predator pack from the east. Ammunition cache compromised. He spoke them aloud. They came true. Around him, soldiers made the sign against evil. Dr. Myles scribbled furiously. Halden’s face collapsed into a single word: threat.

Document.Title: Alien Ruins
Document.Text: Name of Setting Location Role in Story: Related Characters: Season: Unique Features: Description: Sights: Sounds: Smell: Notes:

Document.Title: Commander Veyric Halden
Document.Text: Character Sheet – Commander Veyric Halden Age • Location Late 40s. Senior officer in the Galactic Defense Force, operating from Casper’s Vigil and later dirtside on Virex-9. Role in Story: Human antagonist. Military/corporate-aligned commander who sees the Murnex symbiosis as a potential weapon. Represents humanity’s lust for control and exploitation. Goal: Initial: Contain the Virex-9 situation and bring back alien tech for human advantage. Midpoint: Capture Jake and weaponize his bond as a prototype soldier. Final: Dominate or destroy the symbiote — refuses to accept coexistence. Physical Description: Tall, broad frame, presence fills a room. Trimmed silver hair, square jaw, piercing blue eyes. Uniform polished, medals displayed prominently. Always immaculate. Projects physical and symbolic authority. Personality: Charismatic, commanding, and ruthless. Obsessed with hierarchy and order — sees people as assets. Easily dismisses moral objections if they interfere with results. Has a talent for persuasive speeches that sway subordinates. Occupation: High-ranking GDF commander overseeing multiple survey/military ops. Politically connected; rumored ties to defense contractors who hunger for alien tech. Habits/Mannerisms: Steeples fingers when plotting. Rarely raises his voice; authority comes from controlled tone. Keeps meticulous logs of every action and order — as much for cover as for command. Dislikes physical mess — compulsively wipes hands, uniforms, or gear. Background: Born into a politically ambitious family; military service doubled as a stepping stone. Built a career on “decisive victories,” many of which came at civilian cost. Known in whispers as someone who wins at any price. Believes alien contact is inevitable and must be controlled before it controls humanity. Internal Conflicts: Secret fear: losing control — over subordinates, over situations, over humanity’s future. Loathes unpredictability (Jake + the symbiote embody everything he hates). Convinces himself that ruthlessness is duty, even as cracks of doubt show. External Conflicts: Open conflict with Jake over autonomy vs. control. Tension with Commander Voss — she values protocol, he values results. Distrust of Myles — sees curiosity as weakness, though he covets the scientist’s findings. Notes: Symbol: Humanity’s will to dominate. Arc: From powerful commander → blinded by hubris → downfall when his attempt to control the symbiote causes catastrophe. Catchphrase/Line: “Control is survival. Anything less is surrender.” Fate: Likely downfall in Book One climax — his failure sets the stage for wider human–Murnex conflict in Book Two.

Section 2 — The Ecology of Ink (Awe) They found ruins—arches calcified with black filigree, murals that shifted when you didn’t look straight at them. Harbinger hummed like resonance in a glass. This empire fell because it mistook stillness for safety. We mistake less now. Jake’s fingers trembled without fear for the first time, only wonder.

Document.Title: Dedication
Document.Text: Your dedication here.
Document.Notes: Feel free to delete this document if you don’t need it.

Document.Text: Section 1 — Neither/Nor (Agency) They chained him in a derelict survey pod. Halden argued outside with Dr. Caden Myles, the field biologist who’d begged to study rather than burn. Jake stared at his reflection—human face, inked veins. “I won’t be your weapon,” he told the metal. “And I won’t be your corpse.” There is a third path, Harbinger said. Choose it knowing it narrows every other.

Document.Title: Casper's Vigil (ship)
Document.Text: Name of Setting Location Role in Story: Related Characters: Season: Unique Features: Description: Sights: Sounds: Smell: Notes:

On the walkway over the black pools, Hale joined him. “I still don’t trust it,” he said. “Good,” Jake answered. “Trust isn’t the point. Balance is.” He looked out where the mist turned red at the edges. “It’s always somewhere in between, always.” Inside, Harbinger didn’t argue.

Document.Title: Front Matter
Document.Notes:
Note that in the “Metadata” pane of the Inspector, under “Section Type”, the “Default Subdocument Type” is set to “Front Matter”. This setting causes all subdocuments of this folder to use the “Front Matter” section type by default (that is, when “Structure-Based” is selected as the section type).
This saves us from having to manually assign the “Front Matter” section type for each document we place into this folder.
During Compile, documents assigned the “Front Matter” section type will be laid out differently from documents in the main body of the manuscript.

Document.Text: Chapter Four — The Whisper in the Mind Section 1 — The First Voice (Shock) It began as a pressure behind Jake’s eyes, a tide climbing the shore of his skull. Then a voice formed, not heard but known, syllables arranged like tactics on a board. Host—stabilize respiration. Your fear degrades performance. Jake froze. “Get out of me.” A non-actionable command, the voice replied, dry, amused. We are co-located. Adapt.

Document.Title: Ch 6 - The Gift and the Curse

Document.Text: Section 2 – The Bonding (Loss of Control) The surface erupted. Tendrils of ink lashed upward, clinging with liquid hunger. Gunnach’s rifle clattered to the mud as the substance forced its way beneath his armor, sliding cold and electric into his skin. He fought, clawed, screamed—but the organism was faster, weaving through veins, coiling around nerves. Every instinct screamed violation. He gagged as black veins threaded across his vision. He could feel it rooting in his marrow. It wasn’t just invading his body; it was learning him, cataloguing breath, heartbeat, fear. “Lieutenant!” someone shouted, but their voice blurred, distant, as if through water.

Document.Title: Copyright
Document.Text: Copyright © <$year> <$author> All rights reserved. ISBN: ISBN-13:
Document.Notes: Feel free to delete this document if you don’t need it, or edit it for your needs.

Document.Text: Section 2 — The Rival Draws a Line (Isolation) Sergeant Rourke Hale found him by the field rig. “You should quarantine. For their safety.” “I’m not contagious.” “Could have fooled me when your veins lit up.” Hale’s eyes flicked to Jake’s forearms. “I won’t lose more people to whatever you are now.” Jake wanted to punch, to hug, to beg. Instead he nodded once and felt the ground widen into a gulf beneath his boots.

Document.Title: The Harbinger
Document.Text: Character Sheet – The Harbinger Age • Location Mid-20s to mid-30s (exact age obscured by transformation). Found deep within the marshlands of Virex-9, near ancient Murnex ruins. Role in Story: Secondary antagonist / cautionary figure. The first human (or possibly alien survivor) who bonded fully with a Murnex symbiote and succumbed. Serves as a living prophecy of what Jake could become if he surrenders completely. Goal: Initial: Survive by yielding wholly to the symbiote. Midpoint: Spread the “gift” of union, preaching the inevitability of symbiosis. Final: Serve as mouthpiece for the dormant Murnex Collective. Physical Description: Emaciated yet unnaturally strong. Skin marked by black veins that pulse faintly with bioluminescence. Eyes clouded, distant — sometimes flickering with alien glyph-like patterns. Voice layered, human and alien tones speaking in eerie synchrony. Personality: Unstable, alternating between serene prophet and violent zealot. Speaks cryptically, delivering visions and warnings that blur truth and delusion. Charismatic in their madness — some are drawn in, others repulsed. Occupation: Once a scout or colonist (identity uncertain, lost in assimilation). Now functions as a vessel for fragmented Murnex memories and network whispers. Habits/Mannerisms: Tilts head unnaturally, listening to voices only they can hear. Speaks in riddles, often echoing lines Jake has thought or will think. Claws or scratches symbols into surfaces during episodes. Moves unpredictably: jerky spasms followed by unnatural grace. Background: Disappeared on an earlier survey or expedition to Virex-9. Bonded with a symbiote but lacked Jake’s resilience — gave in fully. Now exists as a half-human, half-Murnex husk, serving as both warning and messenger. Internal Conflicts: None in the human sense — individuality nearly erased. Fragmentary human memories occasionally resurface, creating moments of pain. Trapped between bliss of surrender and agony of fleeting self-awareness. External Conflicts: Opposes Jake by urging him to “let go” and embrace total union. Threat to human crew: spreads panic and temptation. Serves as conduit for Murnex Collective — possibly awakens other symbiotes. Notes: Symbol: The cost of surrender — what happens when the “in-between” balance is lost. Arc: Appears in middle act as both a narrative warning and a rising threat. Defeated/destroyed in Act III to show what Jake refuses to become. Key Line (prophetic): “You fight the tide, Gunnach. But all rivers join the sea.” Fate: Likely destroyed by Jake (with Strategist’s reluctant help) — pivotal moment proving that coexistence must be balance, not surrender.

Section 2 — Barbed Resistance (Struggle) He ran. Branches clawed his armor, mud sucked at his boots, and still the voice matched his stride. He tried to drown it with memory—ship corridors, gravity drills, Rivas’s laugh—then hit a root and pitched forward. Pain lashed up his shoulder. The voice flexed an inner chill and the pain receded. You would lose mobility. Permit repair. “Don’t touch me.” Already done, Lieutenant.

Document.Title: Elara Voss
Document.Text: Character Sheet – Commander Elara Voss Age • Location Early 40s. Aboard Casper’s Vigil, orbiting or near the survey world Virex-9. Role in Story: Supporting human character. Jake’s commanding officer; represents order, discipline, and official protocol. Serves as a foil to Jake’s adaptability and the Strategist’s dominance. Future hook: recipient of Jake’s altered “Gunnach Protocol” message. Goal: Initial: Complete the survey mission of Virex-9 by the book. Midpoint: Contain any threat, even if that means sacrificing personnel. Final: Return home with actionable intel and enforce military order, no matter the moral cost. Physical Description: Tall, composed, regulation-fit. Precise uniform, always immaculate. Sharp gray eyes; hair pulled back into a severe knot. Presence commands authority in a room without theatrics. Personality: Pragmatic, authoritative, skeptical of sentiment. Relentlessly loyal to the chain of command and the mission. Little tolerance for improvisation — especially from subordinates. Beneath the rigidity: a fear of chaos that drives her need for control. Occupation: Commander of the Casper’s Vigil, a Galactic Defense Force survey and scout vessel. Military career built on caution, strict adherence to protocol, and survival through order. Habits/Mannerisms: Speaks in clipped, formal language; avoids unnecessary words. Taps her stylus against her wrist console when impatient. Reviews orders multiple times before delivering them. Never calls subordinates by first names — always rank and last name. Background: Rose through GDF ranks during a series of border conflicts with alien raiders. Earned respect by prioritizing mission success and minimizing civilian casualties, but gained a reputation for being cold. Believes the chain of command is what keeps humanity alive in space. Internal Conflicts: Haunted by past missions where improvisation led to disaster. Deep-seated anxiety about “losing control” — of her crew, of herself, of the mission. Struggles to reconcile compassion for her officers with the cold efficiency demanded of command. External Conflicts: Jake’s defiance and unconventional survival instincts put her authority at risk. The Strategist’s influence over Jake threatens to undermine her trust in him. Political/military oversight: she knows that anything she reports will shape future policy on alien contact. Notes: Symbol: Authority, protocol, and the human military machine. Arc: From skeptical commander → uneasy observer of Jake’s changes → pivotal decision-maker whose interpretation of events sets up Book Two’s conflict. Potential Fate: Remains distant in Book One (ship-bound), but her decision on what Gunnach’s altered broadcast means could either protect or damn humanity in the sequel.

Section 3 — The Pact (Possibility) Under the ruined arch, Dr. Myles drafted terms. “Voluntary hosting. No forced bonds. Shared command: human and host.” Halden stared at the paper a long time. “If this fails—” “It will fail if we don’t try,” Jake said. Halden signed like a man carving a name into his own skin.

Section 3 — The In-Between (Acceptance) He stepped from the pod into the swamp’s metallic dawn. Hale watched from a distance; Halden scowled; Dr. Myles almost smiled. Jake didn’t lift his hands. He didn’t apologize. “I’m here,” he said, voice steady. “I’m still me.” And I am still me, Harbinger added, a private echo. We will see if the world can hold both.

Document.Title: Standard Manuscript
Document.Text:
Andrew Nonymous 700 words. Your manuscript should have your address and contact details on the title page only.
If you have an agent, your agent's address can be included beneath.
NOVEL STANDARD MANUSCRIPT FORMAT by Andrew Nonymous
Nonymous / MANUSCRIPT FORMAT / 1
Chapter One
Standard manuscript format is often required for novels so that work can easily be read by editors. It allows editors to estimate word count and determine roughly how many pages will be required to produce a book using the page size, style and font of their house. This PDF file was generated via Scrivener's compile feature and was created from the "Novel" project template.
Manuscripts for full-length novels require a separate title page. The author's name, address, telephone number and e-mail address should be shown in the upper-left corner of the title page, single-spaced. The title of the story should appear in all caps about halfway down the page. The author's name follows the title on the by-line as the author wants it to appear when published. A real name or pseudonym may be used and may include initials, actual given name or professional designation.
All text should be double-spaced and left-justified with a ragged right margin. Paragraphs should be indented by about five
Nonymous / MANUSCRIPT FORMAT / 2
spaces (half an inch) and not separated by an additional blank line. (It used to be traditional to separate each sentence with two spaces, but in these days of word processors this is now less common.) A 12-point font such as Courier, Times New Roman or Arial should be used throughout. In Scrivener, however, you can write using any font and formatting you choose--the options in compile can be used to change the formatting in the compiled document. In the "Novel" project template, the compile settings will deal with changing the font and formatting to those required for standard manuscript format.
Top, bottom, left and right margins should all be approximately one inch, not allowing for the page header. Each page except for the title page should include a header comprising the author's real surname, the title of the work (or a key word from the title) in capitals, and the page number. In Scrivener, all of this is handled for you by the "Manuscript" compile formats.
# Scene breaks are indicated with the hash character. Do not simply add an extra line space as this can be missed by the typesetter. The "Separators" tab of Scrivener's compile format designer can handle this for you if you are writing each scene as a separate text document. In general, this will be handled by compile in Scrivener, however, so that you can either leave empty lines in the text (and Scrivener will insert the hash character
Nonymous / MANUSCRIPT FORMAT / 3
during compile) or use different documents for different sections (and Scrivener will add the hash between them).
Some editors and publishers prefer emphasis to be indicated by underlining rather than italics. In Scrivener, this conversion can be handled by compile. The "Manuscript (Courier)" compile format will convert italics to underlines, whereas the "Manuscript (Times)" format will not.
Nonymous / MANUSCRIPT FORMAT / 4
Chapter Two
Begin each new chapter on a new page like this with the chapter number about a third of the way down the page. In Scrivener, this can all be dealt with in the compile settings. In Scrivener, all of this is handled automatically by the "Manuscript" compile formats.
Although it is often said that writers should not use the word-count features of their computers to determine the number of words in a manuscript, most publishers and editors will happily accept a word processor word count these days. In Scrivener, you can insert a word count of your manuscript by using Edit > Insert > Draft Word Count (for a manuscript, you may wish to have the word count rounded to the nearest 100, in which case select Edit > Insert > Draft Word Count > Rounded To Nearest 100--this feature is used on the title page of this manuscript, in fact).
Note that the actual word count will not be inserted, but a tag that will be substituted for the word count upon export. In
Nonymous / MANUSCRIPT FORMAT / 5
reality, the formatting of a manuscript is often only really crucial when submitting directly to a publisher--first time novelists will usually submit to an agent first, and agents are often less picky about format and will point you in the right direction before submitting your work to a publisher.
# Much of the information contained in this document is based on a description of novel manuscript format by Matt Carless for the BBC Writers' Room website: http://www.bbc.co.uk/writersroom
# # # # #

Section 3 — Scale (Fear/Anticipation) He climbed a rotten watch spire and saw the swamp as a mesh of currents and minds—predators, plants, people—threads tugging at a knot in his skull. Superintelligence is not a ladder; it is a tide, Harbinger said. “And we’re standing on the shore.” Knee-deep.
Section 3 — Next in the Chain (Existential Dread) “Humans are just… the next host species.” The next partner species—if they choose it. “What if we don’t?” Then your graves will be eloquent.

Section 1 — Operation Purity (Hubris) At dawn, Halden rolled out “de-bonding” rigs—coils, solvents, focused radiation. “Voluntary for the stable. Mandatory for the unstable.” Dr. Myles stood in front of the first host. “You’ll kill them.” “We’re already losing them,” Halden said, voice flat.

Section 2 — Catastrophe (Chaos) The rigs screamed. The unstable host on table three arched, mouth open around a sound no throat could make. The ink tore free of him in a sheet of black light and every bonded spine in the camp lit like wire. The backlash hit—people dropped, convulsing; equipment fried; the swamp itself surged, trees bowing as if to weather. Jake staggered into the blast and threw his body over a thrashing private. “Harbinger—” Redirect. Absorb. Now. They did, together, and the wave broke around them.

Section 2 — Camps Split (Conflict Escalates) By evening there were two fires: one where the “clean” slept, one where Jake and two shaky, newly bonded privates huddled. Their veins glimmered when the sparks rose. The other camp pointed their backs like shields. They fear dilution, Harbinger observed. Identity erosion is a primal terror. Jake rubbed his eyes. “Mine too.”

Chapter Seven — The Gray Network Section 1 — Ghost Signals (Awe) Sleep cracked into fragments of other lives. A woman gasping under floodlights. A boy sprinting across metallic dunes. An old man’s hands stained with living ink. Jake jolted awake, tasting strangers’ fear. Leakage across hosts, Harbinger noted. The network tests its lines. “Network?” A distributed mind. Nascent, wounded, gathering.

Chapter Eight — The Anti-Host Faction Section 1 — Lines in the Mud (Betrayal) Hale assembled a meeting under the canopy. “Hosts risk the unit. We draw a hard line. No shared duties. No shared rations.” He didn’t say Jake’s name. He didn’t have to. Jake waited for someone—anyone—to object. Boots shuffled. No voices rose.

Chapter Three – Unwanted Gift Section 1 – Reflexes Not His Own (Wonder) Jake woke in the swamp’s gray dawn, lungs filling too easily for someone who should have been smoke-scarred. When a vine lashed across his path, his hand moved before his mind could — blade out, severing it mid-swing. His body reacted faster than training ever had. Every sense was heightened: the chirr of insects in the canopy, the vibration of creatures moving under mud, even the distant hum of storm cells forming. It felt like more than human. For a brief moment, awe eclipsed fear.

Chapter Twelve — The Network Awakens Section 1 — The Chorus Arrives (Awe/Horror) Unstable hosts began to hum—low, thrumming, bodies lit with crawling script. The air itself seemed to vibrate. Jake felt the pull again, warmer this time, less like drowning, more like a door opening. A synchronization event, Harbinger said. Stand in it. Do not flinch.

Section 2 — Someone Else’s Memories (Disorientation) During patrol, sunlight flashed on the water and he knew a route through submerged ruins that he had never seen. He stepped where a different body once stepped, heart pounding with a different rhythm. Cross-host recall, Harbinger said. We are—imperfectly—shared. Jake gagged. “Get them out of me.” A thing once learned resists unlearning.

Section 1 — Full Bond (Transformation) In the ruins of a black-filigreed hall, Jake knelt and let Harbinger settle deeper. He didn’t watch from a distance this time; he stepped into the same place inside him where she lived. This is not surrender, she said. “I know.” This is a vow. He rose different—no less himself, but more informed by another mind’s seasons.

Document.Title: Title Page
Document.Text:
<$PROJECTTITLE>
<$author>
Document.Notes: These tags get replaced with the information set in the metadata tab of compile. Alternatively, you can simply replace this text altogether.

Document.Text: Section 3 — Fear in Human Shape (Panic) Halden keyed a secure channel Jake wasn’t meant to hear. “If this… entity can see our moves before we make them, we’re already under occupation.” His fear is arithmetically sound, Harbinger said. But fear is not a plan.

Chapter Six — The Gift and the Curse Section 1 — Saving What Hates You (Admiration) A storage tank ruptured; fire took the west line in seconds. Jake moved before thought. He hauled a tech from a collapsing gantry, charted a path through burning reeds, timed the advance with wind shifts Harbinger fed his muscles like music. When they reached clean air, even Halden’s jaw was clenched in reluctant respect.

Document.Title: Blank Page
Document.Text: <$BLANK_PAGE>
Document.Notes: During compile, Scrivener will try to remove any unnecessary blank pages. The <$BLANK_PAGE> tag tells Scrivener to leave this page blank (the tag itself will be removed from the compiled document).

Document.Text: Section 3 – The Collapse (Horrified Awe) The pain spiked to white fire, then vanished. Gunnach collapsed into the muck, chest heaving, eyes wide. He expected death. Instead, his pulse steadied. His skin sealed where burns had scarred. His vision sharpened, every droplet of mist crystalline in the red light. The ink retreated beneath the surface of his flesh, leaving only faint tracery glowing for a heartbeat before fading. He was alive — only because of it. The swamp whispered again, but this time from inside.

Document.Title: Ch 4 - The Whisper in the Mind

Document.Text: Section 2 – Survival at a Cost (Guilt) Impact tore through steel. The transport clipped treetops, sheared through a ridge, and slammed into methane marsh. Emergency clamps burst open, throwing Marines into the mud and fire. Jake ripped free of his harness, lungs burning. He dragged Private Rivas out of wreckage, but the man’s suit was already torn, flames crawling. Jake fought to smother them, but the methane caught and blossomed. He pulled away as Rivas disappeared in screaming light. Half the squad scattered, some clawing free, others limp in the smoke. The swamp burned blue-white around them, flames dancing across slicks of gas. Jake staggered to his feet, comm crackling static. “Command, this is Gunnach—mission compromised. Multiple casualties.” No reply. Just silence, heavier than the fire. Guilt hit harder than the crash. He’d trusted the drop, trusted Command’s word of “routine,” and now his people were dying in mud and flame. His chest ached, not from bruises but from failure. Hale emerged from the smoke, dragging two Marines by their harnesses. His glare cut through the haze. “This is on you, Lieutenant.” Jake had no strength left to argue.

Document.Title: Dr. Caden Myles
Document.Text: Character Sheet – Dr. Caden Myles Age • Location Mid-30s. Assigned to Casper’s Vigil, part of the Virex-9 survey team. Role in Story: Supporting human character. Expert in xenogenetics; voice of scientific curiosity and ambition. Potential ally to Jake, but also a dangerous wildcard. Future hook: could become the strongest advocate for experimenting with Murnex biology back in human space. Goal: Initial: Study alien biology for knowledge and prestige. Midpoint: Unlock the secrets of Murnex symbiosis. Final: Bring back samples/data — even if it risks human safety — to secure his place in history. Physical Description: Lean build, more scholar than soldier. Slightly disheveled uniform, pockets often stuffed with datapads and specimen vials. Dark eyes that light up when discussing science; often distracted. Personality: Inquisitive, intelligent, restless. Enthusiastic to the point of recklessness. Pragmatic morality: believes knowledge justifies the risks. Charms colleagues with enthusiasm, frustrates superiors with disregard for protocol. Occupation: Xenogeneticist specializing in alien DNA, hybridization, and evolutionary adaptation. Civilian scientist contracted to the Galactic Defense Force for planetary surveys. Habits/Mannerisms: Talks rapidly when excited; loses track of military formalities. Pushes glasses up the bridge of his nose when debating. Tends to absentmindedly hum or tap when analyzing data. Scribbles hypotheses everywhere — datapads, gloves, even walls if necessary. Background: Academic prodigy recruited young into xenogenetics programs. Worked on fringe theories about alien DNA integration before being invited onto Virex-9 survey. Resents military oversight; believes science should drive human progress, not politics. Internal Conflicts: Torn between professional integrity and ambition for recognition. Secret envy of Jake’s forced bond — he wants the same experience but without the loss of autonomy. Struggles with guilt when his curiosity puts others at risk. External Conflicts: Clash with Commander Voss over safety protocols. Distrust from Rourke Hale and other soldiers who see him as reckless. Ethical battle with Jake — is the symbiosis survival or corruption? Notes: Symbol: Humanity’s thirst for knowledge, regardless of cost. Arc: From fascinated observer → reckless advocate → potential architect of future Murnex-human experimentation. Possible Fate in Book One: Survives but compromised — his survival ensures the Murnex threat doesn’t end with Virex-9. Future Role: Catalyst for spreading symbiogenesis into wider human space.

Chapter Nine — The Organism’s Story Section 1 — Origin Fragments (Revelation) During a fever, the swamp peeled back and showed him another world: a sky of amber storms; cities threaded with rivers of script; a war measured in centuries of silence. We did not conquer; we adapted, Harbinger said. Hosts died. Hosts lived. We learned the curve between. “Why me?” Because you hate binaries more than you admit.

Section 3 — Almost Exiled (Desperation) Halden arrived, flanked by MPs. “If med-eval flags you unstable, I will put a rope bridge between you and my people and call it mercy.” “Understood,” Jake said, though nothing in him agreed. He plays at control inside a system that punishes it, Harbinger murmured. Watch; he will overreach.

Document.Title: Jake Gunnach
Document.Text: Character Sheet – Lieutenant Jake Gunnach Age • Location Mid-30s. Assigned to the survey ship Casper’s Vigil, operating in the Virex-9 system. Role in Story: Protagonist. First unwilling host of the Murnex Strategist symbiote. Bridge between humanity and alien intelligence. Goal: Initial: Scout and survive on Virex-9. Midpoint: Resist and contain the symbiote. Final: Adapt, negotiate, and retain humanity while forging fragile coexistence. Physical Description: Athletic, hardened by military service. Dark hair cut regulation-short, a scar under his jaw from a past skirmish. Practical, military-issue uniform and gear — always looks like he’s ready for deployment. Personality: Practical, resilient, stubbornly independent. Natural skepticism, but tempered by curiosity about alien life. Ex-military discipline with flashes of dry humor. Occupation: Exo-biologist & scout officer in the Casper Galactic Defense Force. Specialist in planetary survey missions, first-contact protocols. Habits/Mannerisms: Taps fingers in coded patterns when thinking (leftover from naval comm drills). Checks gear methodically, even in low-stakes situations. Uses clipped, military-style phrasing when under stress. Background: Decorated military officer who transferred to defense force science division. Haunted by an earlier mission where his choices cost lives. Feels more comfortable in fieldwork than bureaucracy. Internal Conflicts: Fear of losing identity and agency to the symbiote. Shame from moments where the Strategist humiliates him, proving she’s smarter. Struggles between military obedience and his own curiosity-driven instincts. External Conflicts: Hostile environment of Virex-9. Commander Voss’s strict adherence to protocol. Antagonism of Marek Rohn / anti-host faction (if he survives). Pressure from the Strategist constantly undermining his control. Notes: Catchphrase: “Adaptation isn’t surrender.” Symbol: The gray zone between human and alien. Arc: From soldier/scout → unwilling captive → adaptive strategist → first bridge to symbiogenesis.

Section 2 — Flight or Capture (Helplessness) They slung him between two carriers like cargo. Harbinger whispered options, but each ended in someone dying. Jake clenched his teeth and took the ride. Staying breathes more data than running, Harbinger said. Endure.

Section 3 — What Am I? (Inner Conflict) Night brought silence. On the edge of camp, with the swamp breathing slow, Jake asked, “Parasite or partner?” You are an asset that prefers consent to compulsion. I can work within that constraint. “I didn’t give consent.” You are alive because I broke protocol. He hated how true that was.

Chapter Five — Shadows of Fear Section 1 — Rumors Like Fire (Suspicion) By sundown, whispers moved faster than the methane flames. “Tainted.” “Ink-sick.” “Black-eyed.” Patrols gave him a wider berth than the swamp’s sinkholes. Jake kept his chin up and his gaze steady, but every stare stuck like burrs in his skin.

Chapter Two – The Living Ink Section 1 – The Black Pool (Curiosity → Dread) The swamp’s silence deepened until Gunnach could hear the throb of his own blood. Ahead, the mud gave way to a depression filled with liquid darker than shadow. The surface rippled in patterns too deliberate to be wind. He crouched, drawn despite the gnaw of dread, and saw faint glimmers under the surface — shapes like script, like letters swimming. One of the remaining soldiers muttered, “It’s ink. A pool of it.” The man’s voice broke, and he stumbled back. Gunnach couldn’t move. Something about the pool wanted him to see. The whispers returned, clear as orders: Come closer.

Section 2 – Blackouts (Fear) But the awe shattered. Gunnach came to standing over a carcass — some predator, its carapace shredded, ichor steaming in the mud. He didn’t remember drawing his weapon. He didn’t remember the fight at all. The others stared at him like he was a stranger. One soldier whispered: “His eyes. They were black.” Terror gripped him harder than the firestorm had. Whatever lived inside him could act without his permission.

Document.Title: Beat Sheet (Book One)
Document.Text:
📘 Beat Sheet for Book One: The First Bond (~90K, 15 chapters)
Act I — The First Touch (Setup, ~30k) Ch. 1 — The Fire Before (~6k) Beat 1: Protagonist in disaster/combat → shows limits of human endurance. (Helplessness) Beat 2: Narrow survival, but cost is heavy (death of comrade or failure of mission). (Guilt) Beat 3: Strange signs of spawn environment hinted (whispers, black residue, unease). (Foreshadowing) Ch. 2 — The Living Ink (~6k) Beat 1: First physical encounter with symbiotic organism. (Curiosity → dread) Beat 2: Organism forcibly bonds, horror of violation. (Loss of control) Beat 3: Collapse, near death → saved only because of it. (Horrified awe) Ch. 3 — Unwanted Gift (~6k) Beat 1: New abilities emerge (senses, healing, reflex). (Wonder) Beat 2: First terrifying blackout → symbiont takes over. (Fear) Beat 3: Protagonist realizes they can’t undo the bond. (Resentment) Ch. 4 — The Whisper in the Mind (~6k) Beat 1: Internal voice emerges — fragmented, alien. (Shock) Beat 2: Protagonist tries to resist → painful backlash. (Struggle) Beat 3: Small cooperation leads to survival in a dangerous moment. (Uneasy trust) Ch. 5 — Shadows of Fear (~6k) Beat 1: Rumors spread of “tainted” humans. (Suspicion) Beat 2: Comrades distance themselves; rival character voices opposition. (Isolation) Beat 3: Public confrontation — protagonist is branded an abomination. (Rejection)
Act II — The In-Between (Confrontation, ~30k) Ch. 6 — The Gift and the Curse (~6k) Beat 1: Protagonist saves lives with spawn powers. (Admiration) Beat 2: Others whisper: “savior” or “monster”? (Division) Beat 3: Protagonist questions identity → parasite or partner? (Inner conflict) Ch. 7 — The Gray Network (~6k) Beat 1: Shared visions across hosts. (Awe) Beat 2: Protagonist feels pieces of others’ memories. (Disorientation) Beat 3: Hint of a vast mind → biological superintelligence. (Fear/anticipation) Ch. 8 — The Anti-Host Faction (~6k) Beat 1: Rival/friend declares hosts dangerous. (Betrayal) Beat 2: Faction grows → camps split into anti-host vs host sympathizers. (Conflict escalates) Beat 3: Protagonist nearly executed/exiled. (Desperation) Ch. 9 — The Organism’s Story (~6k) Beat 1: Symbiont shares fragments of origin — survival instinct, not malice. (Revelation) Beat 2: Protagonist glimpses alien ecology/history. (Awe) Beat 3: Realizes humanity is just next in chain of hosts. (Existential dread) Ch. 10 — When Loyalties Break (~6k) Beat 1: A trusted ally betrays protagonist. (Shock) Beat 2: Protagonist captured / forced to flee. (Helplessness) Beat 3: Symbiont briefly merges them into network — terrifying surge of knowledge. (First awakening)
Act III — Between Worlds (Resolution, ~30k) Ch. 11 — The Host’s Choice (~6k) Beat 1: Protagonist rejects both extremes (eradication & total surrender). (Agency) Beat 2: Consciously negotiates with symbiont. (Partnership) Beat 3: Declares they will live in-between. (Acceptance) Ch. 12 — The Network Awakens (~6k) Beat 1: Network consciousness reveals itself fully. (Awe/horror) Beat 2: Collective predictions and alien logic shown. (Scale increases) Beat 3: Antagonists panic — “It’s not human anymore.” (Fear) Ch. 13 — The Antagonist’s Folly (~6k) Beat 1: Control faction attempts domination/destruction of spawn. (Hubris) Beat 2: Catastrophe — backlash kills/cripples many. (Chaos) Beat 3: Protagonist leads survivors out of collapse. (Leadership) Ch. 14 — The First Accord (~6k) Beat 1: Protagonist fully bonds → balanced symbiosis. (Transformation) Beat 2: Demonstrates new equilibrium to others. (Hope) Beat 3: Seeds first fragile pact between humans & hosts. (Possibility) Ch. 15 — It’s Always Between (~6k) Beat 1: Aftermath: factions shattered, but hope remains. (Bittersweet) Beat 2: Protagonist whispers refrain: “It’s always somewhere in between, always.” (Thematic closure) Beat 3: Final image → dawn of “the in-between age.” (Foreshadowing future books)

Document.Title: Novel and Character Arcs
Document.Text:
Act I — The First Touch (Setup, ~30k words) The Fire Before (~6k) Protagonist in crisis (disaster, combat, expedition). Establish limits of human endurance. Foreshadow strange phenomena. The Living Ink (~6k) First spawn encounter → assimilation scene. Horror/body takeover beats. End: protagonist survives only because of it. Unwanted Gift (~6k) New abilities surface (senses, reflexes, healing). Terrifying loss of control. Hint of whispering consciousness inside. The Whisper in the Mind (~6k) First true contact with symbiont voice. Cryptic, instinctual, alien logic. Raises tension with human comrades. Shadows of Fear (~6k) News of the spawn spreads. Factions begin to form: destroy, study, weaponize. End of Act I: protagonist branded as “tainted.”
Act II — The In-Between (Confrontation, ~30k words) The Gift and the Curse (~6k) Protagonist saves lives with spawn powers. First moral debate: salvation vs corruption. The Gray Network (~6k) Hosts share flashes of collective memory/data. Hint at biological superintelligence. Emotional cost: loss of privacy, loss of “self.” The Anti-Host Faction (~6k) Loved one / rival emerges as anti-symbiosis leader. Escalates external conflict (host vs. human). The Organism’s Story (~6k) Symbiont reveals survival origins. Not conquest → adaptation. Raises question: are humans just the next host species? When Loyalties Break (~6k) Betrayal from inside human camp. Protagonist captured or forced to flee. End: Act II climax — network awakens briefly through them.
Act III — Between Worlds (Resolution, ~30k words) The Host’s Choice (~6k) Protagonist chooses coexistence, not rejection. Begins consciously negotiating with symbiont. The Network Awakens (~6k) First clear emergence of superintelligence. Collective predictions, visions, alien logic. Antagonist faction panics. The Antagonist’s Folly (~6k) Control-seeker faction tries to dominate/destroy spawn. Catastrophic outcome (spawn retaliates/hosts die). Forces protagonist into leadership. The First Accord (~6k) Protagonist forges pact with their symbiont. Demonstrates possibility of balance to others. Seeds future human-spawn coexistence. It’s Always Between (~6k) Aftermath → factions shattered, world forever altered. Protagonist whispers the refrain: “It’s always somewhere in between, always.” Close with dawn of the “in-between age.”
2. Full Character Arcs Protagonist (Reluctant Host) Start: Wants clarity (black/white answers). Feels powerless. Middle: Resists symbiont, fears loss of autonomy. End: Accepts paradox — humanity’s survival is in living between control and surrender. Symbiont (Organism’s Voice) Start: Alien, instinct-driven, seen as parasite. Middle: Begins to reveal motives: survival, not conquest. End: Evolves into a partner — accepts host’s individuality as part of the network. Secondary Human (Anti-Symbiosis Rival) Start: Close to protagonist (friend, sibling, lover, comrade). Represents human fear of corruption. Middle: Becomes leader of anti-host faction. End: Breaks with protagonist, tragically proves “in-between” truth by both being right and wrong. Antagonist (Control Seeker) Start: Military/scientific/corporate figure. Sees spawn only as a tool. Middle: Pushes extremes (weaponization, forced bonding). End: Their downfall demonstrates hubris: symbiosis cannot be forced into binaries.
3. Story Arcs External Conflict Arc Spawn arrive. Humanity splits: salvation vs corruption vs weaponization. Protagonist becomes focal point because they embody the in-between. Climaxes with catastrophe caused by those who try to control/destroy. Internal Conflict Arc Protagonist struggles with identity: self vs symbiont. Fear of losing humanity. Resolution: chooses coexistence → balance over control. Thematic Arc (Superintelligence) Whispers grow into network. By the climax, it’s clear: symbiosis creates a superintelligent collective. Raises Book Two’s central question: can humanity survive being part of a greater mind?

Document.Title: Novel Format
Document.Text:
NOVEL FORMAT About This Template By default, when compiled (File > Compile…), this project will generate a document in the standard manuscript format for novels. Settings are also provided to make it easy to compile to a paperback-style PDF for self-publishing or an ePub or Kindle ebook. How To Use This Template Inside the “Manuscript” folder, create a new folder for each chapter and title each folder with the name of the chapter. If you don’t intend to use chapter names, just use something descriptive that tells you what the chapter is about. (You do not need to—and indeed shouldn’t—title the folders “Chapter One” and so on, because chapter numbering will be taken care of automatically during the Compile process.) The first chapter folder has been created for you with the placeholder title “Chapter”. ⁠Note: The “Manuscript” folder is what we refer to in the documentation as the “Draft folder”. It’s just been renamed “Manuscript” in this template. Create a new text document for each scene inside the chapter folders. (Upon export, scenes will be separated with the “#” character for standard manuscript format and with a blank line for other formats.) Information about characters can be placed in the “Characters” folder, and information about locations can be placed in the “Places” folder. (These are just regular folders that have had custom icons assigned to them using the Documents > Change Icon feature.) Character and setting sketch sheets have been provided which can be used for filling out information about the people and places in your novel. These are located in the “Template Sheets” folder. You should not edit the documents in the “Template Sheets” folder directly unless you wish to change the templates (which you are free to do—you may wish to customise the sketch sheets or get rid of them entirely). To create a new character sheet, click on the Characters folder (or wherever you want to create your new character sheet) and from the Project menu, select New From Template > Character Sketch. This creates a new character sketch document for you to edit and fill in with your character details. You can create setting sketch sheets in the same way. Alternatively, you can just click “Add”, or hit Ctrl+N, with the Characters or Places folders selected. You can view character and setting sketches in the Inspector as follows: Select the “Bookmarks” pane in the Inspector. If necessary, click on “Document Bookmarks” at the top and switch to “Project Bookmarks”. Click on the dropdown arrow next to the “Characters” or “Places” folders in the Bookmarks list and choose the sheet you wish to view. A “Notes” folder has been provided for project-level notes. This has been set as the default Bookmarks folder in Project > Project Settings…, so that when you create a new document from the Bookmarks sidebar in a Quick Reference panel, the document will be added to this folder. Compiling Title pages and front matter are all provided in the “Front Matter” folder (between the “Places” and “Notes” folders). Different front matter is used for different formats.
Tip: You can open this document in a Quick Reference window and have it open alongside the compile dialog if you need to refer to these instructions while compiling. To compile to standard manuscript format Edit the “Title Page” document inside the Front Matter/Manuscript Format folder to ensure that it contains the correct information (by default it will use the Author Information supplied in File > Options…). Go to File > Compile… Next to “Compile for”, select “Print”, “PDF” or one of the rich text file formats such as RTF, Word or OpenOffice. Select either “Manuscript (Courier)” or “Manuscript (Times)” from the list of formats on the left (note that this list changes depending on the file format you have chosen). Ensure that the “Add front matter” button is ticked under the contents list on the right and that the “Manuscript Format” folder is selected in the pop-up button next to it. (This has already been set up for you.) Click on “Compile”. To compile to paperback PDF format Edit the front matter pages contained inside the Front Matter/Paperback folder. Go to File > Compile… Next to “Compile for”, select “PDF”. Select “Paperback (5.06" x 7.81")” from the list of formats on the left. Ensure that the “Add front matter” button is ticked under the contents list on the right and that the “Paperback” folder is selected in the pop-up button next to it. (This has already been set up for you.) Click on “Compile”. To compile to ebook format Edit or remove the dedication page contained inside the Front Matter/Ebook folder. Feel free to add any other front matter documents as required. Import a cover image. You can store this anywhere, but it makes sense to place it in the “Ebook” front matter folder. A placeholder cover image is already provided—you will want to delete that once you have imported your own. Be sure to check online for recommended image sizes and file format, because the recommendations are constantly changing. The sample cover image provided is 2,500 x 1,563 pixels. Go to File > Compile… From the “Compile for” menu, select one of the ebook formats. To create an ePub file, choose either of the “ePub Ebook” formats. To create a Kindle file, choose the “Kindle Ebook” format. Select “Ebook” from the list of formats on the left. If you have front matter documents other than the cover image, ensure that the “Add front matter” button is ticked under the contents list on the right and that the “Ebook” folder is selected in the pop-up button next to it. If you don’t have any front matter documents, you can un-tick “Add front matter”. (Un-ticking this does not affect the cover image.) Above the contents list in the rightmost header bar are six buttons. Click on each of them in turn to go through the various available settings, changing what you need. In particular: Fill in the metadata such as author name and book title. Ensure your cover image is selected and shown. Click on “Compile”. Making Changes There are various minor changes you can make to tweak this template so that it better suits your needs, as follows: Project and author name: The default book title and author name used in headers and elsewhere can be edited in the metadata pane of Compile (click the tag icon in the rightmost header bar of the Compile panel). Chapter subtitles: By default, Compile for this template is set up so that chapter numbers are added but not chapter titles. If you want to include the title of the chapter folder in Compile: Click on “Assign Section Layouts” at the bottom of the Compile panel. Select “Chapter Heading” in the list on the left. Select a different layout on the right, such as “Chapter Title”. (These layouts determine how each part of your manuscript looks in the compiled text.) Font: You can override the font used throughout a compiled document. At the top of the “Section Layouts” pane in the centre of the Compile panel, change the “Font” setting.
Note that fonts are not included in ebooks, however—e-readers choose their own fonts (and you would need to pay a hefty fee for a licence to embed a font in an ebook). The “Font” setting is therefore not available for ebook formats. Page size: The “Paperback” formats are set to create a PDF with a page size of 5.06" x 7.81" or 6" x 9", which are two of the formats you can use with Amazon’s CreateSpace and are popular sizes for trade paperbacks. You can create your own paperback format using a different page size as follows: Right-click on the “Paperback (5.06" x 7.81")” format in Compile’s “Formats” list. Select “Duplicate & Edit Format…”. The compile format designer window will open. In the “Format Name” text field at the top, change the name to something meaningful (e.g. “Paperback (5.5" x 8.5")”) and select “My Formats” as the “Save To” location. Select “Page Settings” in the sidebar. Click on the “Page Setup…” button. In the “Page Setup” panel that appears, ensure “Custom” is selected in the “Paper Size” menu and click the “…” button. Enter a new paper width and height. (Do not worry about setting the margins here; if you want to change these for the compile format, do so after the next step by clicking the “Margins” button just below “Page Setup…”.) Click “OK” and then click “OK” again. Click “Save” in the Compile panel. This will return you to the main Compile preview. You now have a variation of the paperback format using a different page size that you can use at any time with any project. Making your own special folders: The “Characters” and “Places” folders are just regular folders that have been set up in a particular way; you can create other folders that work in a similar manner. To create a folder with a custom icon, add a new folder, place it where you want it, ensure it is selected, and then go to Documents > Change Icon to choose a different icon. To create your own template sheets, create a new document inside the “Template Sheets” folder and set it up however you want (e.g. add text, change the title or icon, set default metadata and so on). Now this document will be available as the basis for creating new documents from the New From Template menu. To make it so that adding a new document to the folder created in (1) always results in a document based on the template set up in (2), select the folder and go to Documents > Default Template for Subdocuments, then choose the template document you created. Working with chapters instead of scenes: By default, this project is set up so that you write each scene as a separate text document. If you don’t like to break things up quite that much and would prefer to write an entire chapter in each text document, make the following changes: Rename the “Scene” document to use your chapter title and move it so that it is on the same level as the “Chapter” folder rather than being inside it. Create a new text document for each chapter. Go to Project > Project Settings… and select “Section Types”. Select the “Default Types by Structure” tab. Delete “Level 1 files” and “Level 2 files and deeper”. In the “Section Type” column, change the setting for “All files” to “Chapter”, then hit “OK”. This tells Scrivener that all your files contain chapter text, not scenes. Compile has already been set up to apply the right formatting to the section types defined here. Using a custom table of contents in ebooks: When exporting to ebook format, Scrivener automatically generates a table of contents. If you would like to customise what appears in the contents, follow these instructions: Create a document for your table of contents inside the Front Matter/Ebook folder. Name the document “Contents”. With the “Contents” document open in the editor, select Navigate > Editor > Lock in Place. The editor header will turn pink to indicate the lock, and you will be able to work in the binder without affecting the editor. In the binder, select the documents you would like to appear in the table of contents (hold down Ctrl to select more than one document). For a simple flat list, drag the selected documents into the editor and drop them in the empty “Contents” document. Alternatively, if you would like the table of contents indented to match the binder structure, select Edit > Copy Special > Copy Documents as Structured Link List and then click into the text of the “Contents” document and hit Ctrl+V or use Edit > Paste. The documents you wish to appear in the table of contents will now appear as a list of links. If you wish to centre the table of contents, select the text and centre it. In the Inspector, change the “Section Type” of the “Contents” document to “Table of Contents”. Remove the editor lock by toggling Navigate > Editor > Lock in Place or deselecting the option from the menu that appears when right-clicking the document icon in the editor header.
Now, when you compile, your custom “Contents” document will be used instead of the automatically-generated one. The titles in the links of the “Contents” document will automatically be updated to match those of the final compiled ebook. Sample Documents The “Sample Output” folder inside the Research folder contains examples of documents that have been created by compiling from this template. “Standard Manuscript” shows the results when compiling to “Manuscript (Courier)”, and “Paperback Novel” shows the results when compiling to “Paperback (5.06" x 7.81")” format with chapter subtitles included, as described in Making Changes, above. Final Note Scrivener project templates are flexible and are not intended to restrict you to a particular workflow. You can change, delete or move the files and folders contained in the template to suit how you work.
Like all templates in Scrivener, this project was originally created from the “Blank” template. We’ve simply added a few folders and set everything up in ways that should be useful to novel writers. Everything you can do with this project, you could equally do by creating a “Blank” project and setting it up yourself.
You can create your own templates by setting up a skeletal project with the files, folders and settings you would like to use for new projects and using File > Save As Template…
